texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Climate change is one of the most challenging environmental problems of our times. More than three quarters of the total amount of greenhouse gases emitted in 2010 was carbon dioxide (CO2) (Edenhofer et al., 2014). Anthropogenic CO2 emissions have increased by 86% from 1980 to 2014 (Boden et al., 2017). Globally, fossil fuel- fi red power plants are the largest anthropogenic sources of CO2 (International Energy Agency, 2016). According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (2017), fossil fuels will likely continue to dominate global power market in the next few decades. Carbon capture and storage is an important strategy to timely control CO2 emissions in order to mitigate climate change (Metz et al., 2005). Post-combustion CO2 capture systems can be retro fi tted into existing power plants, reducing up to 90% of CO2 emissions (Rao and Rubin, 2002). Bene fi cial use of the captured CO2 for enhanced oil recovery has been implemented to o ff set the cost of CO2 capture (van Bergen et al., 2004).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Amino acids are emerging as alternative solvents for absorption-based CO2 capture, but their potential to form the harmful byproducts nitrosamines is not well understood. This study investigated the formation of nitrosamines from model amino acids sarcosine, proline, and taurine under desorberand absorber-relevant conditions. Special attention was paid to the e ff ects of base and the formation of volatile nitrosamines. Under simulated desorber conditions, all three amino acid solvents with organic base monoethanolamine (MEA) formed more total nitrosamines than those with inorganic base sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Increasing base concentration decreased the formation of N -nitrososarcosine (NSAR) in both NaOH- and MEA-based sarcosine solvents. By measuring NSAR formation under varying base concentration and CO2 loading, it was shown that both sarcosine carbamic acid (SARCOOH) and deprotonated sarcosine (SAR -) contributed to NSAR formation. Volatile nitrosamine N -nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) was detected in sarcosine solvents at levels three orders of magnitude lower than NSAR. Kinetic modeling showed that 61% -110% of the accumulated NDMA was formed through NSAR decomposition. NDMA formation from NSAR was promoted by increasing MEA concentration, but suppressed by increasing NaOH concentration. Under simulated absorber conditions, NaOH- and MEA-based solvents formed total nitrosamines at similar rates for sarcosine, proline, and taurine. In sarcosine solvents, NDMA formed at levels three orders of magnitude lower than NSAR, similar to that under desorber conditions.

texts.orig: absorption system, fl ue gas is introduced to the absorber (40 -60 °C) to counter-currently contact with the solvent (e.g., 2.5 -8 M or 15 -50 wt% amine solutions), through which 75 -90% of CO2 is removed (Rao and Rubin, 2002). Treated fl ue gas is emitted to the atmosphere directly or after being scrubbed by a washwater unit. The CO2-rich solvent is pumped to the desorber for regeneration under high temperatures (e.g., 100 -140 °C) (Kuckshinrichs and Hake, 2014), and then circulated back to the absorber through a heat exchanger. The released CO2 is compressed for storage or bene fi cial use. Most commercial solvents are aqueous amine solutions. Monoethanolamine is (MEA) is the benchmark solvent and has been used in an industrial scale CO2 capture facility in Saskatchewan, Canada (Gouedard et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2013).

In the presence of base (i.e., at high pH), the amino group in amino acids exhibits similar a ffi nity towards CO2 as that in their amine analogues (Thee et al., 2014); meanwhile, the carboxyl group, present in the anionic form, substantially reduces the volatility of the molecules (van Holst et al., 2009). Both inorganic (e.g., sodium or potassium hydroxide) and organic bases (e.g., MEA) have been used in amino acid solvents to maintain deprotonation of the amino and carboxyl functional groups (Ciftja et al., 2013). Amino acid solvents potassium prolinate and potassium glycinate showed 2.8 and 1.6 times higher CO2 absorption rate than MEA, respectively (van Holst et al., 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The major environmental impacts of the amine technology are associated with the formation and emission of nitrosamine byproducts. Nitrosamines are formed when NOx in the fl ue gas reacts with amines in the solvent. Additionally, nitrite, a NOx hydrolysis product, reacts with amines in the desorber to form more nitrosamines. Bench-scale studies showed that more nitrosamines were formed by secondary and tertiary amines, such as morpholine, piperazine, triethanolamine, and N -methyldiethanolamine, than by primary amine MEA (Chandan et al., 2013; Dai and Mitch, 2013, 2014; Fine et al., 2014a; Yu et al., 2016). A regulatory example for nitrosamines is the discharge permit issued by the Norwegian Climate and Pollution Agency for the proposed CO2 Technology Centre Mongstad facility, which limited the sum of all nitrosamines and nitramines to 0.3 ng/m 3 in downwind airsheds and 4 ng/L in downwind water supplies (Norwegian Climate and Pollution Agency, 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To date, information has been scarce regarding nitrosamine formation in amino acid solvents. Due to the structural similarity between amino acids and amines, amino acids are expected to also undergo nitrosation on the amino group, but the reactions can be in fl uenced by the carboxyl group or the presence of base in the solvents. This study investigated the kinetics and reaction pathways for nitrosamine formation in amino acid solvents under simulated desorber and absorber conditions. First, three amino acids, sarcosine, L-proline, and taurine that were previously shown to feature relatively high CO2 capture rates (Aronu et al., 2010; Ciftja et al., 2013; Thee et al., 2014; van Holst et al., 2009) were evaluated. Special attention was paid to the e ff ects of base, because it is a crucial component of amino acid solvents. Second, using sarcosine as a model amino acid, the kinetics and mechanisms for nitrosamine formation were investigated. Third, a kinetic model was developed for the formation of the volatile nitrosamine N -nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) in sarcosine solvents. NDMA was selected as a target volatile nitrosamine due to a previous report of its presence in the washwater of a laboratory-scale sarcosine system (Dai and Mitch, 2013). Lastly, nitrosamine formation in the three amino acid solvents was evaluated under simulated absorber conditions.

texts.text: 2.2. Sample preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amino acid solvents for desorber experiments were prepared as mixtures of amino acid, base, sodium bicarbonate, and sodium nitrite. Amino acids were added gravimetrically to achieve the target concentration. Two bases, NaOH and MEA, were evaluated in the experiments. A concentrated NaOH stock solution (8 M) was prepared freshly before use. MEA was added to the amino acid solutions gravimetrically. Sodium bicarbonate was added to obtain the target C/N molar ratios (0 -0.2). A sodium nitrite stock solution (200 mM) was added last to the solvents to obtain the designated initial nitrite concentrations. Each 10 mL of freshly prepared amino acid solvents was transferred to a Swagelok stainless steel tube (6 inch in length, 0.4 inch in inner diameter). The tubes were placed in a convection oven set at the reaction temperature (120 °C). The tubes were removed from the oven at set time points and cooled immediately in a water bath.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the simulated absorber experiments, amino acid solvents were prepared similarly, except that sodium bicarbonate and sodium nitrite were not added. The amino acid solvent (50 mL) was placed in a 125 mL Pyrex ® gas washing cylinder bottle with fritted disc. The bottle was placed in a water bath maintained at 30 °C. The solvent was continuously purged with a stream of synthetic fl ue gas at a fl ow rate of 413 mL/min. The composition of the gas was 10 ppm NO2, 100 ppm NO, 3.2% CO2, 13.6% O2, and 72.6% N2.

texts.text: 2.3. Sample analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nitrite was measured using the standard N -(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine dihydrochloride colorimetric method (Clesceri et al., 1998). Solvent samples were diluted 100 -1000 times with Milli-Q water to obtain nitrite concentrations within the method quanti fi cation range and to minimize interference from amino acids and bases. The diluted sample (10 mL) was mixed with 0.4 mL color reagent. After 10 min, the solution absorbance at 543 nm was measured, which was proportional to the nitrite concentration in the diluted sample.

Brie fl y, solvent samples were pretreated with sulfamic acid (7.0 g/L in the sample) at pH 2 for 24 h to eliminate the interference of nitrite. The pretreated samples were injected into a 60 mL reactor (80 °C) containing a reaction solution constituted by 40 mL of glacial acetic acid and a 4 mL aqueous solution of potassium iodide (48.6 g/L) and iodine (104 g/L). The reaction solution cleaved the N e N bond of a nitrosamine molecule to release a nitric oxide. The nitric oxide in the nitrogen atmosphere was purged through a base trap to remove acidic contaminants, and then into a nitric oxide analyzer (EcoPhysics 88Yet). An NDMA standard solution was used to establish the total nitrosamine calibration curve.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two speci fi c nitrosamines, NSAR and NDMA, were monitored in this study. NSAR was analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a diode array detector (DAD) (Agilent 1260 In fi nity). An Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18 column (4.6 mm × 50 mm, 2.7 μ m) was used with isocratic elution composed of 96% KH2PO4/ H3PO4 solution (pH 2.87) and 4% methanol. The fl ow rate was 1.5 mL/ min and sample injection volume was 5 μ L. The column temperature was maintained at 30 °C. NSAR was detected at 234 nm. NDMA was analyzed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Agilent 7890 B GC, 240 MS) after liquid-liquid extraction with methylene chloride. Deuterated NDMA (d6 -NDMA) was used as the internal standard. The ratio of signals for NDMA and d6-NDMA was used to quantify NDMA concentration in the sample based on a standard curve. Sample was injected in the PTV Solvent Vent mode with 5 μ L injection volume. The initial column temperature was set at 37 °C and held for 0.2 min, and then increased to 40 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min and held for 4.5 min. Finally, the temperature was increased to 250 °C with a rate of 60 °C/ min and held for 1 min. Methanol was used as the chemical ionization reagent.

texts.text: 2.4. Nitrosamine synthesis and characterization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: N -Nitrososarcosine (NSAR) was synthesized based on a procedure modi fi ed from the literature (Kano et al., 1986): 4.45 g sarcosine was fi rst dissolved into 17 mL 6 N hydrochloric acid in a 100 mL Erlenmeyer fl ask. The fl ask was then placed in an ice bath, and a total of 17 mL 6 M sodium nitrite solution was added drop wise to the fl ask over 40 min. The mixture was further stirred for an hour while remaining in the ice bath. The solution was then freeze-dried under reduced pressure. The residue was extracted with 50 mL acetone. The acetone extract was fi ltered by a 0.2 μ m fi lter, then transferred to conical vials and evaporated under a gentle stream of nitrogen gas until an oily residue remained in the vial. NSAR was recrystallized in DCM three times to form pale yellow pellets. The structure of the synthesized NSAR was confi rmed by proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy ( 1 H NMR, Varian Inova-500 spectrometer). The NMR spectra are shown in Fig. 1. The synthesized NSAR generated 90 -120% response in total nitrosamine analysis. The amount of NDMA impurities in the synthesized NSAR was less than 0.7% as determined by GC -MS.

texts.orig: 3.1. Effects of base on nitrosamine formation in amino acid solvents under desorber conditions
texts.text: 3.1. Effects of base on nitrosamine formation in amino acid solvents under desorber conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 2 compares the formation of total nitrosamines in the amino acid solvents using NaOH or MEA as base. For all three amino acids, the MEA-based solvents formed more total nitrosamines than the NaOHbased solvents after 21 h of reaction. The proline-MEA and sarcosineMEA solvents formed 4 -5 times more total nitrosamines than the corresponding NaOH-based solvents. For taurine, the enhancement in nitrosamine formation by MEA was more substantial; the taurine-MEA solvent formed 30 times more total nitrosamines than the taurine-NaOH solvent. Although MEA by itself also formed total nitrosamines, the amount formed (0.01 mM) was insigni fi cant compared with the difference between the NaOHand MEA-based amino acid solvents (13.0 -21.1 mM for proline and sarcosine solvents and 0.31 mM for taurine solvents). These results suggest that MEA, in addition to serving as a base to ensure the deprotonation of amino group, also participated in the nitrosamine formation reactions of amino acids. Fig. 2 also shows that, regardless of the base, proline and sarcosine, both amino acids with a secondary amino group, formed approximately 100 times more total nitrosamines than taurine with a primary amino group. This is consistent with previous fi ndings on the in fl uence of the order of amines on their nitrosamine formation potential, which reported 30 -40-fold higher reactivity of secondary amines than primary amines (Dai and Mitch, 2013; Fine et al., 2014a).

texts.orig: 3.3. N-Nitrososarcosine (NSAR) formation mechanism in sarcosine solvents under desorber conditions
texts.text: 3.3. N-Nitrososarcosine (NSAR) formation mechanism in sarcosine solvents under desorber conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiments were conducted to identify the reactive species in sarcosine solvents to form nitrosamines, and to further explore the possible mechanisms for the enhancement in nitrosamine formation by MEA. Previously, it was shown in a pilot unit that the major nitrosamine product in MEA solvents was N -nitroso-(2-hydroxyethyl) glycine (Einbu et al., 2013), suggesting that N -(2-hydroxyethyl)glycine can be a nitrosamine precursor in MEA-based sarcosine solvent. Additionally, as a primary amine, MEA can form an unstable product upon nitrosation, which rapidly decays to form a carbocation that reacts with another MEA molecule or a sarcosine molecule to form a secondary amine (Yu et al., 2017). In both cases, a nitrosamine precursor other than sarcosine is formed. This, however, contradicts to the observation that NSAR yields from nitrite consumption in MEA-based sarcosine solvents was close to unity across a wide range of conditions (Table 3). Therefore, we further examined the in fl uence of base strength on nitrosamine formation by varying CO2 loading and base concentration (Figs. 7 and 8) to evaluate whether it accounted for the promotion of nitrosamine formation by MEA.

In solvents with a fi xed sarcosine concentration (1 M), increasing MEA concentration from 0 to 4 M decreased NSAR formation from 29.9 to 9.1 mM. NSAR remained the most abundant nitrosamine species (77% -96% of total nitrosamines) regardless of the MEA concentration. texts.orig: In summary, NaOH promoted NSAR formation from SARCOOH by 10 times, while MEA promoted NSAR formation from SAR -by 100 times, both independent from their respective in fl uence on solvent composition. This suggests that the nitrosation product of MEA may serve as an e ff ective transnitrosation agent to SAR -, thereby promoting nitrosamine formation in sarcosine solvents. Further work is needed to evaluate this pathway.

texts.orig: 3.4. Formation of N-Nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) in sarcosine solvents under desorber conditions
texts.text: 3.4. Formation of N-Nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) in sarcosine solvents under desorber conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The carboxyl group in amino acids and their associated nitrosamines ensures the low volatility of these compounds. Previously, NDMA was detected in the washwater of a laboratory-scale absorberwashwater system operated with an NaOH-based sarcosine solvent (2.5 M sodium sarcosinate), suggesting that decarboxylation may occur under absorber conditions (Dai and Mitch, 2013). In the present study, under desorber conditions, NDMA formation was also observed in sarcosine solvents (Fig. 9). In both NaOH- and MEA-based sarcosine solvents, NDMA accumulated steadily over time along with NSAR, although NDMA concentration was approximately 2 -3 orders of magnitude lower than NSAR. After 4.5 h, NDMA concentration in the NaOHand MEA-based solvents was 0.19 μ M and 1.8 μ M, respectively. The 10 times faster NDMA accumulation in the MEA-based solvents could not be attributed to MEA itself, which formed less than 0.1 μ MNDMA (i.e., below detection limit) after 21 h of reaction.

texts.orig: Two pathways may account for NDMA formation in sarcosine solvents. First, the NSAR formed by reactions between sarcosine and nitrite may decarboxylate to form NDMA, similar to that reported in cigarette smoke and food processing (Bharucha et al., 1979; Gray, 1976; Tricker and Preussmann, 1992). Second, sarcosine may decarboxylate to form dimethylamine (Mirvish et al., 1973), which subsequently forms NDMA via reaction with nitrite. To di ff erentiate these two possible pathways, nitrite-free sarcosine solvents were spiked with 1 mM NSAR and subject to the desorber temperature, and the concentration of NDMA was monitored over time (Fig. 10). NDMA formed at rates of 0.25 ± 0.014 and 0.31 ± 0.008 μ Mh -1 in NaOH- and MEA-based solvents, respectively, over the course of 50 h. Because nitrite was absent in these solvents, the only available pathway for NDMA formation was the decomposition of NSAR. Although up to 15 μ M NDMA formed

texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 9, the model prediction agrees well with the observed NDMA formation in the early time period of the experiments, i.e., before 48 h and 2 h for the NaOH- and MEA-based solvents, respectively, suggesting that NDMA was exclusively formed via NSAR decarboxylation. For the fi nal samples collected at 72 h and 4.5 h for the NaOH- and MEA-based solvents, the model underestimated NDMA concentration by 39% and 21%, respectively, suggesting that additional pathways may also contribute to NDMA formation over extended reaction time. Overall, over the course of the experiments, NSAR decarboxylation was the most important pathway for NDMA formation, accounting for 61 -110% and 70 -82% of NDMA accumulation in the NaOH- and MEAbased sarcosine solvents, respectively.

In other words, despite the change in CO2 loading (0 -0.2C/N) and base concentrations (0 -4 M), NSAR decarboxylation remained the dominant pathway for NDMA formation. It is worth noting that transnitrosation (Munro and Williams, 1999) between NSAR and dimethylamine was not evaluated in this study because of the similar basicity between sarcosine (pKa = 10.1) and dimethylamine (pKa = 10.7) (Perrin, 1972), but further research may be needed for veri fi cation.

The ratio between the predicted and measured NDMA concentration ranged from 0.3 -1.9, suggesting that the calculation captured the main NDMA formation pathway. In other words, despite the change in CO2 loading (0 -0.2C/N) and base concentrations (0 -4 M), NSAR decarboxylation remained the dominant pathway for NDMA formation. This fi nding is consistent with the studies of nitrosamines in cooked bacon and cigarette smoke, which reported that the formation of 1-nitrosopyrrolidine primarily proceeded through N -nitrosoproline decomposition when proline was used as the model amino acid (Bharucha et al., 1979; Gray, 1976; Tricker and Preussmann, 1992). It is worth noting that transnitrosation (Munro and Williams, 1999) between NSAR and dimethylamine was not evaluated in this study because of the similar basicity between sarcosine (pKa = 10.1) and dimethylamine (pKa = 10.7) (Perrin, 1972), but further research may be needed for veri fi cation.

texts.orig: 3.5. Nitrosamine formation in sarcosine solvents under simulated absorber conditions
texts.text: 3.5. Nitrosamine formation in sarcosine solvents under simulated absorber conditions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 14 shows the formation of total nitrosamines and nitrite in NaOH- and MEA-based sarcosine, proline, and taurine solvents when purged with a synthetic fl ue gas. Within the experimental period (5 h), taurine solvents did not form detectable amount of total nitrosamines. Sarcosine solvents formed total nitrosamines at rates of 0.048 -0.051 mM h -1 , twice faster than proline solvents (0.022 -0.024 mM h -1 ). The NaOH- and MEA-based solvents behaved similarly. Nitrite accumulation was similar in all solvents, with rates in the range of 0.063 -0.11 mM h -1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After 5 h of reaction, the concentration of NDMA in the NaOH- and MEA-based sarcosine solvents was 0.46 and 0.30 μ M, respectively, whereas the concentration of NSAR was 0.14 mM and 0.21 mM, respectively. The extent of NDMA formation was approximately three orders of magnitude lower than that of NSAR, similar to the relative formation of the two nitrosamines under desorber conditions. These results suggest that the absorber also contributes to NDMA formation, despite its milder reaction condition than the desorber. The Henry ' s Law constant of NDMA is four orders of magnitude higher than that of NSAR (ChemSpider). As a result, despite the low concentration of NDMA formed in the sarcosine solvents, it may be the dominant nitrosamine species in the exhaust gas.

texts.orig: CO2 capture systems. By using sarcosine, proline, and taurine as model amino acids, it was shown that the organic base MEA promoted the formation of total nitrosamines when compared with the inorganic base NaOH. The di ff erence between MEA- and NaOH-based solvents was more pronounced for primary amino acid taurine (30-fold) than for secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline (5-fold). Further experiments using N4444OH and P4444OH as base suggested that base strength was not the only factor contributing to the di ff erence between NaOHand MEA-based solvents. Under the same reaction conditions, secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline formed approximately 100 times more total nitrosamines than the primary amino acid taurine.
texts.text: CO2 capture systems. By using sarcosine, proline, and taurine as model amino acids, it was shown that the organic base MEA promoted the formation of total nitrosamines when compared with the inorganic base NaOH. The di ff erence between MEA- and NaOH-based solvents was more pronounced for primary amino acid taurine (30-fold) than for secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline (5-fold). Further experiments using N4444OH and P4444OH as base suggested that base strength was not the only factor contributing to the di ff erence between NaOHand MEA-based solvents. Under the same reaction conditions, secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline formed approximately 100 times more total nitrosamines than the primary amino acid taurine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second, the kinetics and mechanisms of nitrosamine formation were investigated using sarcosine as a model amino acid. The formation of NSAR, the dominant nitrosamine in sarcosine solvents, was fi rst-order dependent on nitrite concentration. NSAR formation in the MEA-based solvent was not a ff ected by CO2 loading, but that in the NaOH-based solvent increased with CO2 loading. Increasing base concentration inhibited NSAR formation regardless of base. The kinetic modeling showed that although SARCOOH was much more reactive than SAR -in forming NSAR, SAR -contributed comparably, or even more in some cases, to NSAR formation due to its higher concentration.
texts.text: Second, the kinetics and mechanisms of nitrosamine formation were investigated using sarcosine as a model amino acid. The formation of NSAR, the dominant nitrosamine in sarcosine solvents, was fi rst-order dependent on nitrite concentration. NSAR formation in the MEA-based solvent was not a ff ected by CO2 loading, but that in the NaOH-based solvent increased with CO2 loading. Increasing base concentration inhibited NSAR formation regardless of base. The kinetic modeling showed that although SARCOOH was much more reactive than SAR -in forming NSAR, SAR -contributed comparably, or even more in some cases, to NSAR formation due to its higher concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Third, the formation of the volatile nitrosamine NDMA in sarcosine solvents was investigated under desorber conditions. NDMA accumulated at levels 2 -3 orders of magnitude lower than NSAR. Modeling showed that NDMA formation from NSAR decomposition accounted for the majority of NDMA accumulated in the sarcosine solvents. NDMA formation exhibited a fi rst-order dependence on NSAR concentration. Varying CO2 loading did not a ff ect NDMA formation from NSAR. Increasing base concentration increased NDMA formation in MEAbased sarcosine solvents, but decreased NDMA formation in NaOHbased solvents.
texts.text: Third, the formation of the volatile nitrosamine NDMA in sarcosine solvents was investigated under desorber conditions. NDMA accumulated at levels 2 -3 orders of magnitude lower than NSAR. Modeling showed that NDMA formation from NSAR decomposition accounted for the majority of NDMA accumulated in the sarcosine solvents. NDMA formation exhibited a fi rst-order dependence on NSAR concentration. Varying CO2 loading did not a ff ect NDMA formation from NSAR. Increasing base concentration increased NDMA formation in MEAbased sarcosine solvents, but decreased NDMA formation in NaOHbased solvents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lastly, the formation of nitrosamines in amino acid solvents was evaluated under simulated absorber conditions. There was no di ff erence observed between NaOH- and MEA-based solvents. Similar to the trends observed under desorber conditions, secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline formed at least 100 times more nitrosamines than the primary amino acid taurine. NDMA was also detected in sarcosine solvents, at levels three orders of magnitude lower than NSAR.
texts.text: Lastly, the formation of nitrosamines in amino acid solvents was evaluated under simulated absorber conditions. There was no di ff erence observed between NaOH- and MEA-based solvents. Similar to the trends observed under desorber conditions, secondary amino acids sarcosine and proline formed at least 100 times more nitrosamines than the primary amino acid taurine. NDMA was also detected in sarcosine solvents, at levels three orders of magnitude lower than NSAR.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Volatile nitrosamines, despite their trace concentrations, are of particular concern for amino acid solvents due to their high emission potential. The fi ndings of this study suggest that using NaOH as base is bene fi cial for limiting the formation of total nitrosamines and suppressing the formation of volatile nitrosamines from the decarboxylation of nitrsoamino acids.
